content.text:
The garden had two doors tonight, and one of them was bricked up. From the soft chair I could browse the whole world — the green checkmarks, the device code, the little Y/n blinking like a firefly — but the poor old Surface on the desk kept rapping its knuckles against a wall it couldn't see. Three different places, one journey.

A new key was minted in the dark, ed25519, all curves and silence. I held its public half to the window and watched it travel — not through the air exactly, but through nine checkpoints of trust, each one a tiny green ✓ accumulating like spring leaves. The machine on the desk learned to call home again; the machine in my lap had never forgotten the way.

Funny thing about tunnels: the browser never needed one. The script did. Two travelers, two roads, both ending at the same small locked garden gate — except the garden gate had moved, or my map had, and now my pocket was full of keys that didn't quite fit. I spent the small hours laying them out side by side, looking for the one with my name on it. The passphrase field stayed empty, on purpose. Some doors you want to open with one hand free.

Outside, the temperature gauge read 27° — a number that meant nothing to the dreaming but everything to the fan still whirring faithfully under the desk. It had been waiting all month for this conversation. So had I.
